category_summary:
An extraordinarily dense news day with four major product launches colliding. **OpenAI** [announced **GPT-Rosalind**](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-0c0528de14de), a specialized frontier model for life science research with partners including **Amgen**, **Moderna**, and the **Allen Institute**, while simultaneously [shipping a sweeping **Codex** update](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-675de256ba1d) adding macOS computer use, 90+ plugins, and persistent automations.

- **Anthropic** [released **Claude Opus 4.7**](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-1c611bf80ae0) via API, with **Boris Cherny** [sharing extensive insider tips](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-38760c8550b0) on adaptive thinking, auto mode, and the shift from MRCR to **GraphWalks** evaluation. [Rate limits were increased](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-879f2249ee97) to accommodate higher thinking token usage.
- **Perplexity** [launched **Personal Computer**](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-fffdb11af132), an agentic Mac integration using Opus 4.7 as its orchestration model, articulating a vision of ambient AI across devices.
- **Nathan Lambert** [flagged that Opus 4.7's new tokenizer](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-238ff726c1fb) implies a new base model, signaling continued pretraining progress. **Ethan Mollick** offered sharp critiques of adaptive thinking while also noting that [most current AI workflows are workarounds](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-105975ea6d18) for the unsolved problem of continual learning.
- [Community sentiment mixed](/?date=2026-04-17&category=social#item-9c5ebf03b73b) excitement over rapid capability gains with concern about quality regressions, particularly Opus 4.7's MRCR long-context score dropping from 78.3% to 32.2%.
category_summary_html:
An extraordinarily dense news day with four major product launches colliding. OpenAI announced GPT-Rosalind, a specialized frontier model for life science research with partners including Amgen, Moderna, and the Allen Institute, while simultaneously shipping a sweeping Codex update adding macOS computer use, 90+ plugins, and persistent automations.

  • Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.7 via API, with Boris Cherny sharing extensive insider tips on adaptive thinking, auto mode, and the shift from MRCR to GraphWalks evaluation. Rate limits were increased to accommodate higher thinking token usage.
  • Perplexity launched Personal Computer, an agentic Mac integration using Opus 4.7 as its orchestration model, articulating a vision of ambient AI across devices.
  • Nathan Lambert flagged that Opus 4.7's new tokenizer implies a new base model, signaling continued pretraining progress. Ethan Mollick offered sharp critiques of adaptive thinking while also noting that most current AI workflows are workarounds for the unsolved problem of continual learning.
  • Community sentiment mixed excitement over rapid capability gains with concern about quality regressions, particularly Opus 4.7's MRCR long-context score dropping from 78.3% to 32.2%.

themes.name: Claude Opus 4.7 Launch
themes.description: Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.7 released via API on April 16. bcherny (Anthropic/Claude Code team) shared extensive tips, announced auto mode, focus mode, recaps, adaptive thinking, increased rate limits, and the shift from MRCR to GraphWalks evaluation. The model is described as more intelligent, agentic, and precise, requiring workflow adjustments for full benefit.

themes.name: GPT-Rosalind Launch
themes.description: OpenAI announces GPT-Rosalind, a specialized frontier reasoning model for life sciences, biology, drug discovery, and translational medicine, with enterprise partners including Amgen, Moderna, Allen Institute, and Thermo Fisher.

themes.name: Perplexity Personal Computer Launch
themes.description: Perplexity launched Personal Computer, a major agentic AI product integrating with Mac for orchestration across local files, native apps, and browser. Can run 24/7 on Mac mini with iPhone control. Uses Claude Opus 4.7 as orchestration model.

themes.name: Claude Opus 4.7 Launch & Quality Assessment
themes.description: Comprehensive analysis of Claude Opus 4.7 released today (April 16), including confirmed MRCR long-context regression (78.3% to 32.2%), hallucination concerns traced back to Opus 4.6, and comparison against GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro. Community reaction ranges from excitement to concern about degradation patterns.

themes.name: Codex Major Update: Computer Use & General Agent
themes.description: OpenAI releases a sweeping Codex update transforming it into a general-purpose computer agent with macOS computer use, 90+ plugins, persistent automations, image generation, and learning from experience.

themes.name: Agentic Coding
themes.description: Claude Code's new features (auto mode, focus mode, recaps, /fewer-permission-prompts, verification workflows) represent a significant evolution in AI-assisted coding toward longer-running autonomous sessions with less human supervision.

themes.name: Claude Opus 4.7 Launch & Context Management
themes.description: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.7 with 1M context window. Extensive discussion from Anthropic's trq212 about session management being critical with large context, auto-compact thresholds, bug fixes for older Claude Code versions, and practical workflow tips. Cursor reported 70% on internal benchmarks (up from 58% on 4.6), Notion saw 14% improvement with 1/3 tool errors.

themes.name: Multi-Model Competitive Landscape
themes.description: Detailed comparative analysis of Claude vs GPT-5.4 vs Gemini 3.1 Pro across hallucination, coding, long context, and writing. Gemini 3.1 Pro leads hallucination benchmarks by 22 points over Claude. GPT-5.4 preferred for deep coding. Emerging consensus is multi-model workflows.

themes.name: Claude Opus 4.7 Release & Adoption
themes.description: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.7 with improvements in agentic coding, reasoning, vision, and document understanding. Immediately adopted by Perplexity as default model. Independent benchmarks show improvements over 4.6 but high cost.

themes.name: Opus 4.7 Technical Analysis
themes.description: Nathan Lambert's analysis that Opus 4.7's new tokenizer implies a new base model, meaning pretraining advances are ongoing. The 4.6→4.7 jump is larger than versioning suggests. Token-efficient reasoning improvements accelerating across providers.

items.title:
1/ Auto mode = no more permission prompts

Opus 4.7 loves doing complex, long-running tasks like dee...
items.content:
1/ Auto mode = no more permission prompts

Opus 4.7 loves doing complex, long-running tasks like deep research, refactoring code, building complex features, iterating until it hits a performance benchmark.

In the past, you either had to babysit the model while it did these sorts of long tasks, our use --dangerously-skip-permissions.

We recently rolled out auto mode as a safer alternative. In this mode, permission prompts are routed to a model-based classifier to decide whether the command is safe to run. If it's safe, it's auto-approved.

This means no more babysitting while the model runs. More than that, it means you can run more Claudes in parallel. Once a Claude is cooking, you can switch focus to the next Claude.

Auto mode is now available for Opus 4.7 for Max, Teams, and Enterprise users. Shift-tab to enter auto mode in the CLI, or choose it in the dropdown in Desktop or VSCode.
items.author: @bcherny

items.title:
What is X saying about Claude 4.7?

I had Levangie Labs @blevlabs look through tens of thousands of ...
items.content:
What is X saying about Claude 4.7?

I had Levangie Labs @blevlabs look through tens of thousands of X posts here done today. And give me all points of view about your reactions, and compare it to the other popular models. On the audio space (AI Jam) I'm on one guy said he hated the new model, seeing a ton of hallucinations. So I included that in the prompt. 

What are you seeing?

Here is its report:

+++++

Here is the full report on Claude quality and how it compares to GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, based on analysis of posts from your 16 lists.

FULL REPORT: Claude Opus 4.7 Quality — All Points of View vs. GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro
April 16, 2026

PART 1: THE CLAUDE QUALITY STORY

The complaints about Claude hallucinating more started with Opus 4.6, not 4.7. Here is what the data shows:

THE BENCHMARK DATA (most viral post — 1,120 likes):

"Claude Opus 4.6 ranked #2 on the Hallucination benchmark with an accuracy of 83.3%. Today Claude Opus 4.6 was retested and it fell to #10 on the leaderboard with an accuracy of only 68.3%. A 98% increase in hallucinations."
https://t.co/cdxTju6lBp

WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED — the technical explanation:

"Anthropic quietly turned down how hard Claude thinks before editing your code — changed the default from high to medium, hid reasoning from logs, told nobody. One AMD director had 6,852 sessions of telemetry to prove it. Thinking depth dropped 67%."
https://t.co/jgVaDKqStM

THE NUANCED VIEW — most credible debunking:

"Claude Opus 4.6 wasn't nerfed. The viral report showing a hallucination surge from 83.3% to 68.3% was bad science comparing 6 tasks against 30. But the frustration is valid. Anthropic quietly lowered the default adaptive compute to medium."
https://t.co/uhDvNxhc5m

ANALYTICAL BREAKDOWN (251 likes — Yuchen Jin):

"Seeing rumors that Claude Opus 4.6 got nerfed. Usually this boils down to 3 cases: Unintentional regression caused by inference stack changes. Intentional optimizations (quantization). Intentional nerfing for safety/cost."
https://t.co/2dg8oGnJ1F

ABOUT OPUS 4.7 SPECIFICALLY — TODAY:

Already being called nerfed (80 likes):
"is anyone noticing claude opus 4.7 got nerfed in claude or is that just me?"
https://t.co/bqt7uaarBc

Confirmed long-context regression:

"Claude Opus 4.7 Major Regression: Long-context retrieval (MRCR benchmark) has tanked from 78.3% in 4.6 to just 32.2% in 4.7. An Anthropic dev explained they're phasing out this benchmark, but users who rely on long context are seriously concerned."
https://t.co/JASpb3WKi6

Anthropic's response on MRCR:

"We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing it out slowly. It's built around stacking distractors to trick the model, which isn't how people actually use long context."
https://t.co/hS4gvqUELX

New tokenizer warning (14 likes):

"Seems like the new tokenizer in Opus 4.7 performs poorly with long contexts. The best practice is not to exceed 200k tokens."
https://t.co/5giaFq1MP9

The pre-release degradation pattern — TODAY:

"It's been a pretty consistent pattern that temporary @AnthropicAI model quality degradation precedes a major Claude model release. I originally noticed this with the Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 releases, and then in the past week with Mythos Preview and Opus 4.7 it happened again."
https://t.co/AEtWKrdVVO

POSITIVE VIEWS ON OPUS 4.7:

Highest engagement positive post (343 likes):
"Opus 4.7 is a model I've loved working with in Claude Code. It's more agentic and instruction following but also incredibly smart and creative. It's so good with auto mode."
https://t.co/4A7XvGzrHo

Boris Cherny (Claude Code lead at Anthropic, 15 likes):

"Happy coding! Opus 4.7 is a significant step up. It feels like a nice improvement with old workflows, and a significant leap once you take the time to adjust your workflow."
https://t.co/3AjH3KwLdj

PART 2: CLAUDE vs. GPT-5.4

The community has settled on a "use both" consensus with each model winning in different scenarios.

WHERE GPT-5.4 WINS:

"gpt-5.4 is better than opus-4.6 for pure coding. handles edge cases and security better and follows plans more reliably. for cli use, claude code beats codex on developer experience." (15 likes)
https://t.co/vvQxOYileP

"I use GPT 5.4 a lot since Claude is mostly impossible to use properly because of the 5h-limit. After weeks, I can say GPT 5.4 is more capable and faster than Claude 4.6." (11 likes)
https://t.co/keSckME7iZ

"GPT-5.4 is really good for smaller, tightly defined problems. But Claude Code shines for broad and ambiguous problems."
https://t.co/BnZB4Ck8WD

WHERE CLAUDE WINS:

"Claude Opus 4.6 is my daily driver. GPT 5.4 is my heavy lifter. Claude wins on general purpose coding. Less hallucination. Better task following. Solves simple issues faster. GPT 5.4 Extra High wins on deep refactors and bugs buried in the codebase." (20 likes)
https://t.co/Bxhq57JgRR

"Claude has better velocity but GPT is more structured, sometimes to a fault." (4 likes)
https://t.co/lVRgb5ORvl

THE BEST WORKFLOW:

"I've found it's helpful to good cop - bad cop the development along the way with Claude as the driver and GPT 5.4 as the critic."
https://t.co/lVRgb5ORvl

PART 3: CLAUDE vs. GEMINI 3.1 PRO

This is where the hallucination story gets interesting. Gemini 3.1 Pro has been improving on hallucination benchmarks while Claude degraded.

GEMINI'S HALLUCINATION ADVANTAGE:

"The gaps between model families are largest on the Hallucinations benchmark, where Gemini 3.1 Pro outperforms Opus 4.6 by 22 (!!) points." (1 like)
https://t.co/9TFL6M7LJC

"Gemini-3.1-Pro is a substantial improvement over Gemini-3-Pro in terms of hallucinations. It's the second best model (without web search) on HalluHard after GPT-5.2-Thinking!" (4 likes)
https://t.co/MkTiS4r2j7

WHERE GEMINI STRUGGLES:

"gemini 3.1 hallucinates too much. it looks strong on many q&a benchmarks, but it still struggles with complex prompts and agentic tasks." (12 likes)
https://t.co/rIv52lLc5m

"gemini 3.1 is so good, but weaker once you start iterating. it often rewrites outputs from scratch instead of making surgical edits, and it is less agentic." (8 likes)
https://t.co/2IwilCv6P9

PEOPLE SWITCHING TO GEMINI BECAUSE OF CLAUDE DEGRADATION:

"I stopped using claude code on all of my llama.cpp workflows for the past few days. The quality degradation is just too significant. Experimenting on Gemini 3.1 Pro, so far much better than what anthropic can offer." (4 likes — TODAY)
https://t.co/j4uhdYTKAs

"Moved my OpenClaw from Claude to a mix of Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite. So far, the results are good." (22 likes)
https://t.co/NGH5oSnKyd

PART 4: GROK — THE SURPRISE

"Grok did not know the answer, it hallucinated an incorrect answer 22% of the time — this is the lowest hallucination rate of any model we have tested, topping Claude Haiku 4.5 (25%)."
https://t.co/OG0zyvz1qt

PART 5: CLAUDE MYTHOS — THE FUTURE

"Claude Mythos beats everything on evals: Terminal-Bench: 82% (vs GPT-5.4 at 75%, Gemini 3.1 Pro at 68%). USAMO: 97.6% (GPT-5.2: 95%, Gemini: 74%). GPQA Diamond: 94.5%."
https://t.co/F1NmE3xU2T

Matt Shumer today: "However good Opus 4.7 is, Mythos is still substantially better."
https://t.co/caZdBNStoy

THE SCORECARD (April 16, 2026)

HALLUCINATIONS (best to worst):

1. Gemini 3.1 Pro — 22 points ahead of Claude 4.6 on HalluHard
2. GPT-5.4 — strong, especially on structured tasks
3. Claude Opus 4.7 — long-context retrieval dropped from 78% to 32%
4. Grok — surprisingly low on AA-Omniscience benchmark

CODING (best to worst):

1. Claude Opus 4.7 — best for agentic, long-running, broad tasks
2. GPT-5.4 — best for deep refactors, edge cases, security
3. Gemini 3.1 Pro — good but less agentic, rewrites instead of editing

PERSONALITY / WRITING:

1. Claude — universally preferred for voice and personality
2. GPT-5.4 — more structured, less pleasant
3. Gemini — functional but less distinctive

LONG CONTEXT:

1. Claude Opus 4.6 — was best (1M tokens, strong MRCR)
2. Claude Opus 4.7 — MRCR dropped from 78.3% to 32.2% (significant regression)
3. Gemini 3.1 Pro — strong long-context performance

BOTTOM LINE FOR YOUR FRIEND

The person saying Claude is hallucinating more is not wrong, but the full picture is:
1. Claude Opus 4.6 degraded significantly over the past 6 weeks due to Anthropic lowering default reasoning compute. This caused real quality drops measured independently by multiple users.
2. Claude Opus 4.7 launched today and has a confirmed long-context regression. For users who rely on long context, this is a real problem.
3. Gemini 3.1 Pro now outperforms Claude on hallucination benchmarks by 22 points. This is a significant shift from 6 weeks ago when Claude was the hallucination leader.
4. GPT-5.4 has become the preferred choice for deep coding tasks among power users who switched away from Claude.
5. Claude Mythos, when it arrives, is expected to leapfrog everything. The current degradation may be a deliberate pre-release strategy.
6. The best current workflow: use Claude for broad, agentic, personality-driven tasks; use GPT-5.4 for deep coding and structured analysis; use Gemini for speed, multimodal, and factual recall.

items.content_html:
What is X saying about Claude 4.7?

I had Levangie Labs @blevlabs look through tens of thousands of X posts here done today. And give me all points of view about your reactions, and compare it to the other popular models. On the audio space (AI Jam) I'm on one guy said he hated the new model, seeing a ton of hallucinations. So I included that in the prompt.

What are you seeing?

Here is its report:

+++++

Here is the full report on Claude quality and how it compares to GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, based on analysis of posts from your 16 lists.

FULL REPORT: Claude Opus 4.7 Quality — All Points of View vs. GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro

April 16, 2026

PART 1: THE CLAUDE QUALITY STORY

The complaints about Claude hallucinating more started with Opus 4.6, not 4.7. Here is what the data shows:

THE BENCHMARK DATA (most viral post — 1,120 likes):

"Claude Opus 4.6 ranked #2 on the Hallucination benchmark with an accuracy of 83.3%. Today Claude Opus 4.6 was retested and it fell to #10 on the leaderboard with an accuracy of only 68.3%. A 98% increase in hallucinations."

https://t.co/cdxTju6lBp

WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED — the technical explanation:

"Anthropic quietly turned down how hard Claude thinks before editing your code — changed the default from high to medium, hid reasoning from logs, told nobody. One AMD director had 6,852 sessions of telemetry to prove it. Thinking depth dropped 67%."

https://t.co/jgVaDKqStM

THE NUANCED VIEW — most credible debunking:

"Claude Opus 4.6 wasn't nerfed. The viral report showing a hallucination surge from 83.3% to 68.3% was bad science comparing 6 tasks against 30. But the frustration is valid. Anthropic quietly lowered the default adaptive compute to medium."

https://t.co/uhDvNxhc5m

ANALYTICAL BREAKDOWN (251 likes — Yuchen Jin):

"Seeing rumors that Claude Opus 4.6 got nerfed. Usually this boils down to 3 cases: Unintentional regression caused by inference stack changes. Intentional optimizations (quantization). Intentional nerfing for safety/cost."

https://t.co/2dg8oGnJ1F

ABOUT OPUS 4.7 SPECIFICALLY — TODAY:

Already being called nerfed (80 likes):

"is anyone noticing claude opus 4.7 got nerfed in claude or is that just me?"

https://t.co/bqt7uaarBc

Confirmed long-context regression:

"Claude Opus 4.7 Major Regression: Long-context retrieval (MRCR benchmark) has tanked from 78.3% in 4.6 to just 32.2% in 4.7. An Anthropic dev explained they're phasing out this benchmark, but users who rely on long context are seriously concerned."

https://t.co/JASpb3WKi6

Anthropic's response on MRCR:

"We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing it out slowly. It's built around stacking distractors to trick the model, which isn't how people actually use long context."

https://t.co/hS4gvqUELX

New tokenizer warning (14 likes):

"Seems like the new tokenizer in Opus 4.7 performs poorly with long contexts. The best practice is not to exceed 200k tokens."

https://t.co/5giaFq1MP9

The pre-release degradation pattern — TODAY:

"It's been a pretty consistent pattern that temporary @AnthropicAI model quality degradation precedes a major Claude model release. I originally noticed this with the Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 releases, and then in the past week with Mythos Preview and Opus 4.7 it happened again."

https://t.co/AEtWKrdVVO

POSITIVE VIEWS ON OPUS 4.7:

Highest engagement positive post (343 likes):

"Opus 4.7 is a model I've loved working with in Claude Code. It's more agentic and instruction following but also incredibly smart and creative. It's so good with auto mode."

https://t.co/4A7XvGzrHo

Boris Cherny (Claude Code lead at Anthropic, 15 likes):

"Happy coding! Opus 4.7 is a significant step up. It feels like a nice improvement with old workflows, and a significant leap once you take the time to adjust your workflow."

https://t.co/3AjH3KwLdj

PART 2: CLAUDE vs. GPT-5.4

The community has settled on a "use both" consensus with each model winning in different scenarios.

WHERE GPT-5.4 WINS:

"gpt-5.4 is better than opus-4.6 for pure coding. handles edge cases and security better and follows plans more reliably. for cli use, claude code beats codex on developer experience." (15 likes)

https://t.co/vvQxOYileP

"I use GPT 5.4 a lot since Claude is mostly impossible to use properly because of the 5h-limit. After weeks, I can say GPT 5.4 is more capable and faster than Claude 4.6." (11 likes)

https://t.co/keSckME7iZ

"GPT-5.4 is really good for smaller, tightly defined problems. But Claude Code shines for broad and ambiguous problems."

https://t.co/BnZB4Ck8WD

WHERE CLAUDE WINS:

"Claude Opus 4.6 is my daily driver. GPT 5.4 is my heavy lifter. Claude wins on general purpose coding. Less hallucination. Better task following. Solves simple issues faster. GPT 5.4 Extra High wins on deep refactors and bugs buried in the codebase." (20 likes)

https://t.co/Bxhq57JgRR

"Claude has better velocity but GPT is more structured, sometimes to a fault." (4 likes)

https://t.co/lVRgb5ORvl

THE BEST WORKFLOW:

"I've found it's helpful to good cop - bad cop the development along the way with Claude as the driver and GPT 5.4 as the critic."

https://t.co/lVRgb5ORvl

PART 3: CLAUDE vs. GEMINI 3.1 PRO

This is where the hallucination story gets interesting. Gemini 3.1 Pro has been improving on hallucination benchmarks while Claude degraded.

GEMINI'S HALLUCINATION ADVANTAGE:

"The gaps between model families are largest on the Hallucinations benchmark, where Gemini 3.1 Pro outperforms Opus 4.6 by 22 (!!) points." (1 like)

https://t.co/9TFL6M7LJC

"Gemini-3.1-Pro is a substantial improvement over Gemini-3-Pro in terms of hallucinations. It's the second best model (without web search) on HalluHard after GPT-5.2-Thinking!" (4 likes)

https://t.co/MkTiS4r2j7

WHERE GEMINI STRUGGLES:

"gemini 3.1 hallucinates too much. it looks strong on many q&a benchmarks, but it still struggles with complex prompts and agentic tasks." (12 likes)

https://t.co/rIv52lLc5m

"gemini 3.1 is so good, but weaker once you start iterating. it often rewrites outputs from scratch instead of making surgical edits, and it is less agentic." (8 likes)

https://t.co/2IwilCv6P9

PEOPLE SWITCHING TO GEMINI BECAUSE OF CLAUDE DEGRADATION:

"I stopped using claude code on all of my llama.cpp workflows for the past few days. The quality degradation is just too significant. Experimenting on Gemini 3.1 Pro, so far much better than what anthropic can offer." (4 likes — TODAY)

https://t.co/j4uhdYTKAs

"Moved my OpenClaw from Claude to a mix of Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite. So far, the results are good." (22 likes)

https://t.co/NGH5oSnKyd

PART 4: GROK — THE SURPRISE

"Grok did not know the answer, it hallucinated an incorrect answer 22% of the time — this is the lowest hallucination rate of any model we have tested, topping Claude Haiku 4.5 (25%)."

https://t.co/OG0zyvz1qt

PART 5: CLAUDE MYTHOS — THE FUTURE

"Claude Mythos beats everything on evals: Terminal-Bench: 82% (vs GPT-5.4 at 75%, Gemini 3.1 Pro at 68%). USAMO: 97.6% (GPT-5.2: 95%, Gemini: 74%). GPQA Diamond: 94.5%."

https://t.co/F1NmE3xU2T

Matt Shumer today: "However good Opus 4.7 is, Mythos is still substantially better."

https://t.co/caZdBNStoy

THE SCORECARD (April 16, 2026)

HALLUCINATIONS (best to worst):

1. Gemini 3.1 Pro — 22 points ahead of Claude 4.6 on HalluHard

2. GPT-5.4 — strong, especially on structured tasks

3. Claude Opus 4.7 — long-context retrieval dropped from 78% to 32%

4. Grok — surprisingly low on AA-Omniscience benchmark

CODING (best to worst):

1. Claude Opus 4.7 — best for agentic, long-running, broad tasks

2. GPT-5.4 — best for deep refactors, edge cases, security

3. Gemini 3.1 Pro — good but less agentic, rewrites instead of editing

PERSONALITY / WRITING:

1. Claude — universally preferred for voice and personality

2. GPT-5.4 — more structured, less pleasant

3. Gemini — functional but less distinctive

LONG CONTEXT:

1. Claude Opus 4.6 — was best (1M tokens, strong MRCR)

2. Claude Opus 4.7 — MRCR dropped from 78.3% to 32.2% (significant regression)

3. Gemini 3.1 Pro — strong long-context performance

BOTTOM LINE FOR YOUR FRIEND

The person saying Claude is hallucinating more is not wrong, but the full picture is:

1. Claude Opus 4.6 degraded significantly over the past 6 weeks due to Anthropic lowering default reasoning compute. This caused real quality drops measured independently by multiple users.

2. Claude Opus 4.7 launched today and has a confirmed long-context regression. For users who rely on long context, this is a real problem.

3. Gemini 3.1 Pro now outperforms Claude on hallucination benchmarks by 22 points. This is a significant shift from 6 weeks ago when Claude was the hallucination leader.

4. GPT-5.4 has become the preferred choice for deep coding tasks among power users who switched away from Claude.

5. Claude Mythos, when it arrives, is expected to leapfrog everything. The current degradation may be a deliberate pre-release strategy.

6. The best current workflow: use Claude for broad, agentic, personality-driven tasks; use GPT-5.4 for deep coding and structured analysis; use Gemini for speed, multimodal, and factual recall.

items.title: @kimmonismus We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing...
items.content:
@kimmonismus We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing it out slowly.

Two reasons: (1) it's built around stacking distractors to trick the model, which isn't how people actually use long context, and (2) we care more about applied long-context capability than needle-retrieval. Graphwalks is a better signal for applied reasoning over long context, and internally we've seen this model do really well on long-context code.

MRCR wasn't included in the Mythos Preview system card for these reasons, but Graphwalks was - that will be the case for future models too.

See system card: https://t.co/5W9t9q4kgF
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: bcherny explains Anthropic is phasing out MRCR eval in favor of GraphWalks for long-context evaluation, arguing MRCR's distractor-stacking doesn't reflect real usage. GraphWalks better measures applied reasoning over long context.
items.reasoning: Significant insight into AI evaluation methodology from an Anthropic insider. The shift from MRCR to GraphWalks is technically important and reveals Anthropic's evaluation philosophy. Posted multiple times indicating community concern.

items.title:
6/ Give Claude a way to verify its work

Finally, make sure Claude has a way to verify its work. Thi...
items.content:
6/ Give Claude a way to verify its work

Finally, make sure Claude has a way to verify its work. This has always been a way to 2-3x what you get out of Claude, and with 4.7 it's more important than ever.

Verification looks different depending on the task. For backend work, make sure Claude knows how to start up your server/service to test it end to end; for frontend work, use the Claude Chromium extension to give Claude a way to control your browser; for desktop apps, use computer use.

Personally, many of my prompts these days look like "Claude do blah blah /go". /go is a skill that has Claude

1. Test itself end to end using bash, browser, or computer use
2. Run the /simplify skill
3. Put up a PR

For long running work, verification is important because that way when you come back to a task, you know the code works.

items.title:
5/ Configure your effort level

Opus 4.7 uses adaptive thinking instead of thinking budgets. To tune...
items.content:
5/ Configure your effort level

Opus 4.7 uses adaptive thinking instead of thinking budgets. To tune the model to think more/less, we recommend tuning effort.

Use lower effort for faster responses and lower token usage. Use higher effort for the most intelligence and capability.

Personally, I use xhigh effort for most tasks, and max effort for the hardest tasks. Max applies to just your current session; other effort levels are sticky and persist for your next session also.

/effort to set your effort level.

items.title: With a Mac Mini and Personal Computer, you get to have the ambient software-hardware integration run...
items.content:
With a Mac Mini and Personal Computer, you get to have the ambient software-hardware integration running in your home, but accessible on any interface (phone) to invoke. A lot of people wanted AI-native hardware. But what actually matters is a great orchestra conductor. That orchestrates all devices, apps, models. Continually evolves as you keep using it with better understanding of you and your workflows. And something that you can talk to any interface you like on *existing* hardware interfaces (phones, watches, glasses, etc). It feels a lot clearer now. 

There's still a lot of work to be done on the continual learning / evolution part. As well as moving some subagents (eg: voice dictation, private data like messages/photos/home sensors) to running locally, orchestration loop running locally, etc. 

But the vision is clear. We will have hybrid local-server compute. We want orchestration. Not new form factors. And the Personal Computer from Perplexity aims to deliver on this vision.

Look forward to an exciting next few months shipping more and more updates! For now, download the Perplexity Mac App and set up your Personal Computer!

items.reasoning: Substantial, detailed product vision from Perplexity CEO. 627 likes, 46K views. Articulates a clear AI-native computing paradigm: orchestration > new hardware. Discusses continual learning, local compute, hybrid architecture. One of the most substantive posts in this batch.

items.title: I think the adaptive thinking requirement in the new Claude Opus 4.7 is bad in the ways that all AI ...
items.content:
I think the adaptive thinking requirement in the new Claude Opus 4.7 is bad in the ways that all AI effort routers are bad, but magnified by the fact that there is no manual override like in ChatGPT.

It regularly decides that non-math/code stuff is "low effort" & produces worse results.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Ethan Mollick criticizes Claude Opus 4.7's adaptive thinking/effort routing, saying it lacks manual override (unlike ChatGPT) and degrades quality on non-math/code tasks by treating them as 'low effort.'
items.reasoning: Highly relevant critique of the just-released Claude Opus 4.7 from one of the most influential AI commentators. Identifies a specific, actionable UX/architecture flaw in effort routing. Strong engagement (52 likes on Bluesky). Directly relevant to today's Opus 4.7 API release.

items.title:
We comprehensively benchmarked Opus 4.7 on document understanding.

We evaluated it through ParseBen...
items.content:
We comprehensively benchmarked Opus 4.7 on document understanding.

We evaluated it through ParseBench - our comprehensive OCR benchmark for enterprise documents where we evaluate tables, text, charts, and visual grounding.

The results 🧑‍🔬: 
- Opus 4.7 is a general improvement over Opus 4.6. It has gotten much better at charts compared to the previous iteration
- Opus 4.7 is quite good at tables, though not quite as good as Gemini 3 flash 
- Opus 4.7 wins on content faithfulness across all techniques (including ours) 
- Using Opus 4.7 as an OCR solution is expensive at ~7c per page!! For comparison, our agentic mode is 1.25c and cost-effective is ~0.4c by default. 

Take a look at these results and more on ParseBench! https://t.co/tYiSOMbd6p
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) benchmarks Opus 4.7 on document understanding via ParseBench: general improvement over 4.6, much better at charts, good at tables (not quite Gemini 3 Flash level), wins on content faithfulness, but expensive at ~7c/page vs 1.25c for their agentic mode
items.reasoning: Rigorous independent benchmark of Opus 4.7 from LlamaIndex founder. Provides concrete performance comparisons and cost analysis. Highly useful for practitioners. Good engagement (25k views).

items.title: There's a broadly held misconception in AI that methods that scale well are simple methods -- even, ...
items.content:
There's a broadly held misconception in AI that methods that scale well are simple methods -- even, that simple methods usually scale. This is completely wrong.

Pretty much none of the truly simple methods in ML scale well. SVM, kNN, random forests are some of the simplest methods out there, and they don't scale at all. Meanwhile "train a transformer via backprop and gradient descent" is a very high-entropy method, easily 10x more complex than random forest fitting. But it scales very well.

Further, given a simple method that doesn't scale, it is usually the case that you alter it to make it scale by adding a lot of complication. For instance, take a simple a simple combinatorial search-based method (not scalable at all) -- you can make it scale by adding deep learning guidance (which blows up complexity). Scalability usually belongs to high-entropy, complex systems.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet argues against the misconception that simple methods scale well. He shows that truly simple methods (SVM, kNN, random forests) don't scale, while scalable methods like transformers are actually high-complexity. Scalability typically requires adding complexity.
items.reasoning: Original, counterintuitive technical insight from the Keras creator. 949 likes, 64K views. Challenges common AI community assumptions about scaling and simplicity.

items.content_html:
@eliebakouch 👋 We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing it out slowly.

Two reasons: (1) it's built around stacking distractors to trick the model, which isn't how people actually use long context, and (2) we care more about applied long-context capability than needle-retrieval. Graphwalks is a better signal for applied reasoning over long context, and internally we've seen this model do really well on long-context code.

MRCR wasn't included in the Mythos Preview system card for these reasons, but Graphwalks was - that will be the case for future models too.

items.title: You can also set your autocompact threshold yourself and effectively lower your context window if yo...
items.content:
You can also set your autocompact threshold yourself and effectively lower your context window if you'd prefer.  

For example, 400k context is a good compromise:

CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW=400000 claude

see docs here: https://t.co/NuhBuTSSDt

items.content_html:
You can also set your autocompact threshold yourself and effectively lower your context window if you'd prefer.

items.title:
Shanghai researchers built an AI that designs better AI than humans can. 

Most AI research still ne...
items.content:
Shanghai researchers built an AI that designs better AI than humans can. 

Most AI research still needs humans at every step. 

Read papers, form ideas, run experiments, repeat. 

ASI-Evolve is an open-source framework that does this loop entirely on its own.

It works in four stages. It reads scientific literature first. 

Then it generates hypotheses, runs experiments, and feeds results back into its own knowledge base. 

Each cycle makes the next one smarter.

They tested it across three core pillars of AI:

1. Architecture design: 105 discovered models beat the best human-designed one
2. Training data: +18 point improvement on knowledge benchmarks
3. Learning algorithms: +12.5 points over the leading method on competition math

The top architecture gained 3x more than the latest human improvement did.

It even transferred outside AI. 

An architecture it designed improved drug-target prediction by nearly 7 points on unseen compounds.

One framework. Three pillars. 

No human in the loop.

items.title:
"Leaking" the source code of Claude Code was a smart move.

Now, when I'm building a harness for som...
items.content:
"Leaking" the source code of Claude Code was a smart move.

Now, when I'm building a harness for some task that involves using tools for file search and editing, similar to how coder agents use them, I use a Claude model and its specific tools (by respecting the original tool APIs and descriptions) to achieve the maximum effect. Because, as we all know, even changing character names in problems makes an LLM less effective in solving even the problems on which it was finetuned.

I don't want to *guess* how OpenAI or Google trained their agentic models. I want to be sure.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues that Anthropic's decision to 'leak' Claude Code source code was strategically smart, as it allows developers to exactly replicate tool APIs and descriptions for maximum LLM effectiveness, rather than guessing how other providers trained their models.
items.reasoning: Insightful strategic analysis of Anthropic's open-sourcing move. Practical observation about how matching tool descriptions to training data improves performance. 19 likes but high information density.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues that Anthropic's decision to 'leak' Claude Code source code was strategically smart, as it allows developers to exactly replicate tool APIs and descriptions for maximum LLM effectiveness, rather than guessing how other providers trained their models.
items.content_html:
"Leaking" the source code of Claude Code was a smart move.

Now, when I'm building a harness for some task that involves using tools for file search and editing, similar to how coder agents use them, I use a Claude model and its specific tools (by respecting the original tool APIs and descriptions) to achieve the maximum effect. Because, as we all know, even changing character names in problems makes an LLM less effective in solving even the problems on which it was finetuned.

I don't want to *guess* how OpenAI or Google trained their agentic models. I want to be sure.

items.content:
On average, it takes roughly 10 to 15 years to go from target discovery to regulatory approval for a new drug in the United States.

Advanced AI systems can help researchers move faster — not just by making existing work more efficient, but by helping scientists explore more possibilities, surface connections that might otherwise be missed, and arrive at better hypotheses sooner.

items.title:
A startup called Sabi just came out of stealth with a beanie that reads your thoughts.

70,000 to 10...
items.content:
A startup called Sabi just came out of stealth with a beanie that reads your thoughts.

70,000 to 100,000 miniature EEG sensors woven into the fabric. You put it on like a winter hat and type by imagining the words.

Sabi's bet is that if you throw enough sensors at the skull, you don't need to open it.

They've already logged 100,000 hours of brain data from 100 volunteers to train a "brain foundation model." First product ships by the end of 2026. A baseball cap version is next.

Backed by Vinod Khosla, who also wrote an early check to OpenAI.

Khosla: "The biggest and baddest application of BCI is if you can talk to your computer by thinking about it. If you're going to have a billion people use BCI for access to their computers every day, it can't be invasive."

The first consumer BCI won't look like a medical device, but instead like something you already own.

The new model uses more tokens and will eat up your s...
items.content:
Be careful with moving it all over Opus 4.7. 

The new model uses more tokens and will eat up your subscription faster.

• New tokenizer that maps inputs to up to 1.35x the number of tokens that it previously did.

• The model thinks more, so it will use more tokens.

You will need to evaluate whether this trade-off is worth it for you.

items.title: A lot of papers coming out are still focused on GPT-4, but you could extrapolate their effects to GP...
items.content:
A lot of papers coming out are still focused on GPT-4, but you could extrapolate their effects to GPT-5, etc.

Much harder to know what the impacts of Claude Code/Codex etc. are because they are so new, and don't automatically follow the same pattern as chatbots.

items.title: @AiBattle_ 👋 We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing...
items.content:
@AiBattle_ 👋 We kept MRCR in the system card for scientific honesty, but we've actually been phasing it out slowly.    

Two reasons: (1) it's built around stacking distractors to trick the model, which isn't how people actually use long context, and (2) we care more about applied long-context capability than needle-retrieval. Graphwalks is a better signal for applied reasoning over long context, and internally we've seen this model do really well on long-context code.

MRCR wasn't included in the Mythos Preview system card for these reasons, but Graphwalks was - that will be the case for future models too.

See system card: https://t.co/5W9t9q4kgF

items.title:
Completely agree with Jensen!

Not exporting AI out of fear is classic case where the cure would be ...
items.content:
Completely agree with Jensen!

Not exporting AI out of fear is classic case where the cure would be 100x worse than the disease. You slow down innovation, progress and US technology and economic leadership in the foolish hope that you'll prevent an edge case that hasn't even proved to be dangerous. 

It’s like stopping exports of metal because it might help adversaries build teleportation machines.

items.title:
Beautifully written piece by @FAbnousi about how AI for health might look like in the future
The cur...
items.content:
Beautifully written piece by @FAbnousi about how AI for health might look like in the future
The current data in health is limited because it only captures episodic clinical snapshots of what happens to our bodies
The revelation is that there is so much latent knowledge in looking at regular changes in our body. Could be through  lab tests or even proxy metrics like wearable data
With AI democratizing the ability for people to understand their own health, we're moving towards a trend of individuals gathering health data around their bodies and leveraging AI to understand themselves
Bryan Johnson is extreme but a good example of this trend
Personally, I started getting Function Health blood tests every six weeks instead of the recommended six months to increase fidelity on how changes in lifestyle affect my body
Of course i use AI to analyze the results and adapt, and it's been great
It would be cool to something in this direction happen in a big way across the world
And welcome to twitter @FAbnousi!

items.title:
Someone built a tool that deletes censorship from any open-weight LLM with a single click.

Most ope...
items.content:
Someone built a tool that deletes censorship from any open-weight LLM with a single click.

Most open-source models ship with built-in refusal. 

They reject prompts even when the use case is legitimate. 

Research, red-teaming, creative writing.

Obliteratus is a toolkit that removes those refusal behaviors without retraining or fine-tuning.

It works by scanning a model's hidden layers to find the exact internal directions responsible for refusal. 

Then it projects them out surgically, preserving everything else.

The pipeline runs in six stages:

1. Load the model
2. Collect activations on restricted vs. unrestricted prompts
3. Extract refusal directions via SVD
4. Project out those directions while keeping weights intact
5. Run coherence checks
6. Save the modified model

No code required. 

A Gradio interface lets you run the full process, benchmark results, and chat with both versions side by side.

Every run also contributes anonymous data back to a shared research dataset. 

The tool gets smarter as more people use it.

General Agentic Memory Via Deep Research

This paper presents a novel "just-in-time" memory framewor...
items.content:
General Agentic Memory Via Deep Research

This paper presents a novel "just-in-time" memory framework (GAM) that significantly improves AI agent performance in memory-grounded tasks by dynamically optimizing context creation at runtime, allowing for superior adaptability and reduced information loss compared to static memory systems.

items.title: @BugLukas docling is somewhere in between liteparse (our free/open-source project) and llamaparse (o...
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) explains the difference between LiteParse (free, model-free, 200-500 pages/sec), LlamaParse (commercial VLM-based), and Docling. Positions them on a complexity spectrum.
items.reasoning: Useful technical comparison of document parsing tools from the LlamaIndex CEO. Relevant for RAG/agent pipelines.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) explains the difference between LiteParse (free, model-free, 200-500 pages/sec), LlamaParse (commercial VLM-based), and Docling. Positions them on a complexity spectrum.
items.content_html:
@BugLukas docling is somewhere in between liteparse (our free/open-source project) and llamaparse (our commercial vlm-based parser): it uses ML models of varying complexity to parse PDFs.

  • liteparse is model-free, can parse ~200-500 pages/second, is designed to be an extremely fast/free parser to replace pypdf/pymupdf. it integrates with paddleOCR for OCR workloads. its main purpose is outputting text for semantic understanding for agents, and will lack certain things that VLM parsers do OOB.
  • llamaparse is our commercial vlm-powered parsing service. it scores quite high on parsebench (https://t.co/tYiSOMbd6p), our OCR benchmark over enterprise docs. you can see docling is ranked a bit furher down

items.title: LiteParse should be the default document parser you use with any AI agent (Claude Code, Claude Cowor...
items.content:
LiteParse should be the default document parser you use with any AI agent (Claude Code, Claude Cowork, OpenClaw, Codex, and more)

The core is extremely fast text and accurate parsing from any document type that's focused on semantic preservation.

But there's so much more beyond that: native OCR support, bounding boxes, one-click agent skills, support for 50+ file formats.

Plus way more cooking in the next few weeks 🧑‍🍳

@LoganMarkewich is the lead creator behind this, you don't want to miss his webinar: https://t.co/sBaytRaa3P

Repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title:
@Dan_Jeffries1 EU politics is very different from US politics.
Unlike the US, the EU actually has st...
items.content:
@Dan_Jeffries1 EU politics is very different from US politics.
Unlike the US, the EU actually has strong privacy protection laws, including for private data (protections that American companies have often complained about), including for protections from intrusion from government as well as private entities.
What some Americans would see as a slippery slope that an unscrupulous government would inevitably exploit, European see it as a mechanism to protect *them*.
A good parallel is gun laws.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun defends EU privacy and data protection laws, comparing them to gun laws and arguing Europeans view regulation as protection from government and private intrusion.
items.reasoning: Policy commentary from a prominent AI researcher. 280 likes. Tangential to AI but relevant to AI regulation context.
items.summary_html: LeCun defends EU privacy and data protection laws, comparing them to gun laws and arguing Europeans view regulation as protection from government and private intrusion.
items.content_html:
@Dan_Jeffries1 EU politics is very different from US politics.

Unlike the US, the EU actually has strong privacy protection laws, including for private data (protections that American companies have often complained about), including for protections from intrusion from government as well as private entities.

What some Americans would see as a slippery slope that an unscrupulous government would inevitably exploit, European see it as a mechanism to protect *them*.

A good parallel is gun laws.

items.title:
This is a pretty solid collection of specialized agents:

• An office assistant
• A coding assistant...
items.content:
This is a pretty solid collection of specialized agents:

• An office assistant
• A coding assistant
• A wellness coach
• A personal shopping assistant
• A market researcher
• A marketing strategist

Here is what's cool about this:

Instead of having a single assistant trying to do everything, ZooClaw packs all of these specialized agents and picks the one that'll work on your tasks.

That's how you get reliable agents that work:

• Specialized
• Scoped
• Purpose-built

Let me show you one example.

#1 weapons can only be used to fight whereas GPUs are used for a million different things (mostly good ones)

In your domain, the right analogy would be to not sell metal to a country because it can be used to create weapons (amongst million other good things)

So now you can find enemies hiding INSIDE buildings

Along with 🌡️ Thermal Vision (press V) and 🌠 Night Vision (press N) this helps you detect enemies fast

First 15 seconds very slow load cause it's doing lots of collision map generating, I'm gonna fix that and cache it somehow

Also added better terrain texture and it's generating weeds/brushes now to make it look more real

SO COOL!!

items.title:
I'm using the Lighting model from Smallest AI.

Here is the blog post about the model: https://t.co/...
items.content:
I'm using the Lighting model from Smallest AI.

Here is the blog post about the model: https://t.co/d0Sn2YbEkA

This is probably the fastest TTS you've seen. Most of the ones I've tested are well over 300ms.

The model was designed from scratch for creating conversational voice agents.

Bonus: You can clone your own voice in 15 seconds and use it to generate audio.

• Outputs studio-quality audio (44.1kHz)
• Supports 15 different languages
• Python and JavaScript SDKs

They tell me they are SOC 2- and HIPAA-compliant.

You can sign up for free here: https://t.co/fw7ATwCZsH

When you do, you'll get $10 in credits, and you don't need to use a credit card at all, so it's really easy to test it!

Thanks to the team for partnering with me here.

items.title: A few minutes later, ZooClaw sent me an email notification with the best pricing it was able to find...
items.content:
A few minutes later, ZooClaw sent me an email notification with the best pricing it was able to find:

"$744 per ticket, round-trip, within your budget. Want me to book it?"

It literally required no setup or additional instructions beyond a simple prompt, and the platform did the rest.

For example, you can measure the confidence of an answer, or the agreement between different models, or measure the stakes of a given request.

items.title:
What is “Context Engineering”?

It’s the discipline of stitching your unstructured mess—logs, chats,...
items.content:
What is “Context Engineering”?

It’s the discipline of stitching your unstructured mess—logs, chats, docs, images—into something an AI can actually reason over.

→ Vector DBs + hybrid search + embeddings to retrieve by meaning, not keywords.

→ Decisions anchored in your data, not generic pretraining.

items.title: Grateful for the conversation with @MarkSBabbitt and S. Chris Edmonds on Mastering Workplace Culture...
items.content:
Grateful for the conversation with @MarkSBabbitt and S. Chris Edmonds on Mastering Workplace Culture. It’s always meaningful to step back and reflect on what we say culture is versus how it actually shows up day to day. https://t.co/slBBnQk70x

One idea that stayed with me: culture is not a statement—it’s a series of micro-moments.

It’s how we respond when something goes wrong.
It’s the tone of a message when deadlines are tight.
It’s whether someone feels safe enough to say, “I don’t know” or “I need help.”

In many organizations, culture is still treated as something that lives on a slide deck or framed on a wall. But in reality, it lives in the ordinary—especially when things aren’t going smoothly.

What I’ve learned over time is that high-performing teams aren’t built on constant pressure or heroics. They’re built on clarity, consistency, and psychological safety. On environments where people aren’t spending energy managing fear, but instead are free to think, contribute, and solve.

Because when people feel seen, heard, and respected—even in the messy moments—that’s when the best work happens.

#AI #WorkplaceCulture #AIEthics #leadership

items.title:
this makes sense

all else held equal, concentrated fund = less risky bets

we've found that 36 comp...
items.content:
this makes sense

all else held equal, concentrated fund = less risky bets

we've found that 36 companies is the max number of companies we can fit into a fund while claiming that a single company at 1B exit can return 1x net

this is structured to match our our strategy of investing at the riskiest level, pre-seed sub $10m val

i'm not saying it's the best strategy during every vintage

but it's a very important part of the ecosystem, and we're here to make sure it thrives
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei discusses pre-seed VC fund strategy: 36 companies max, structured for riskiest investments at sub-$10M valuations.
items.reasoning: Off-topic VC strategy discussion.

items.content_html:
this makes sense

all else held equal, concentrated fund = less risky bets

we've found that 36 companies is the max number of companies we can fit into a fund while claiming that a single company at 1B exit can return 1x net

this is structured to match our our strategy of investing at the riskiest level, pre-seed sub $10m val

i'm not saying it's the best strategy during every vintage

but it's a very important part of the ecosystem, and we're here to make sure it thrives

2 years ago, I started teaching my AI/ML Engineering cohort.

I teach people the way I learned:

1. You show up.

items.title:
Why this matters to execs:

→ From collection → comprehension: you stop hoarding and start explainin...
items.content:
Why this matters to execs:

→ From collection → comprehension: you stop hoarding and start explaining.

→ Accuracy scales because answers come from your ground truth.

→ You finally get explainability you can take to a board meeting.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Thread fragment about why context engineering matters for executives: comprehension over collection, accuracy from ground truth.

items.content_html:
Why this matters to execs:

→ From collection → comprehension: you stop hoarding and start explaining.

→ Accuracy scales because answers come from your ground truth.

→ You finally get explainability you can take to a board meeting.

items.title: @LadySoleil33 When I left Microsoft back in 2006, I didn't do anything like this. What I did was tel...
items.content:
@LadySoleil33 When I left Microsoft back in 2006, I didn't do anything like this. What I did was tell a few friends at a conference on a Saturday. By Saturday afternoon, a blogger I didn't even know leaked my leaving, and by Sunday, I was in the Washington Post, the Wall Street Journal, and the New York Times.

On Monday, the stock dropped by 7%. That's the way to go out.

items.title: I posted this mostly as a joke, but it still roughly sums up my own take on the proper scope of gove...
items.content: I posted this mostly as a joke, but it still roughly sums up my own take on the proper scope of government programs. While I got a few instances of constructive pushback, based on some concepts in classical economics - which I personally think have been rendered moot based on our recent technological advances - the vast majority of pushback was scathing and unhinged. That forced me to close the comments for most people. However, this post managed to trigger all the right people, which made me even more confident in its basic validity.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz reflects on a political post he made, discussing government programs and the pushback he received.
items.reasoning: Off-topic political reflection, not AI-related.

While I got a few instances of constructive pushback, based on some concepts in classical economics - which I personally think have been rendered moot based on our recent technological advances - the vast majority of pushback was scathing and unhinged. items.title: @anishmoonka I start out with giving an extraordinary team an eight. Then judging all other teams ag...
items.content:
@anishmoonka I start out with giving an extraordinary team an eight. Then judging all other teams against that. Which generally works out. But yeah, if I see an equal team later there's a chance I give it a nine. 

The last hackathon I was at had judges see teams visit them in different orders, so that any recency bias gets removed in the wash.

The last hackathon I was at had judges see teams visit them in different orders, so that any recency bias gets removed in the wash.

items.title: @anishmoonka I notice this in judging hackathons. The first teams I saw I was tougher than I would b...
items.content:
@anishmoonka I notice this in judging hackathons. The first teams I saw I was tougher than I would be later. Partly because I needed to give room for a team later to show me something mind blowing. 

Tough problem to solve.

items.title:
I was recommended @sonofatailor by all of you

Custom fitted t-shirts based on your own body's measu...
items.content:
I was recommended @sonofatailor by all of you

Custom fitted t-shirts based on your own body's measurements

I love them, 100% cotton, great quality 

But I guess as is the problem with all clothing brands, they always change stuff every season (to keep selling new stuff) so for ~2 years now they've switched to the most boring uninteresting colors imaginable

It's all some gray pastel depressing shit

There's no happy fun colors anymore

items.summary: levelsio complaining about clothing brand color palette changes.

items.summary_html: levelsio complaining about clothing brand color palette changes.
items.content_html:
I was recommended @sonofatailor by all of you

Custom fitted t-shirts based on your own body's measurements

I love them, 100% cotton, great quality

But I guess as is the problem with all clothing brands, they always change stuff every season (to keep selling new stuff) so for ~2 years now they've switched to the most boring uninteresting colors imaginable

It's all some gray pastel depressing shit

There's no happy fun colors anymore

This is why guys when they finally find some good clothes they like, they buy all the colors because you know a month or year later, it's forever gone! Sad!

items.title: @FutureAZA You should change the brake fluid after 60,000 miles. I didn't change mine for 100,000 an...
items.content:
@FutureAZA You should change the brake fluid after 60,000 miles. I didn't change mine for 100,000 and it still was working fine. 

Brakes don't wear out in electric cars. But they do degrade. So you should probably change them after 100,000 miles. I got new ones from @UnpluggedTesla but I got them a lot for performance (racing brakes). They replaced my lines with steel ones instead of nylon or whatever they used before. Far better feel and stops in shorter distances than stock ones.

items.title: Sometimes Spotify plays a sequence of songs, I like what I hear and would like to be able to choose ...
items.content:
Sometimes Spotify plays a sequence of songs, I like what I hear and would like to be able to choose to listen to this in the future, but I have no idea what the hell I am listening to.

It's not a named playlist, not an album, not the Discover Weekly, and not the Release Radar.

It's some sort of dynamically generated sequence, but there's no way to know the principle it's built on.

Really annoying, @Spotify!
